name: Explain this project
description: Walk through the project the user is in, then write an ABOUT.md summarising it.
prompt:
Introspect the project I'm in right now and produce an ABOUT.md that a new contributor (human or AI) could read in 5 minutes to understand the shape of the codebase.

Do this carefully:

1. `ls $SYNAPSE_FILES` first to see anything I've uploaded. If a README, PROGRESS, ARCHITECTURE, or AGENTS file exists there or in the repo root, start by reading those -- don't restate what they already say.
2. Walk the top-level directory tree (limit depth ~3) and group folders by role (entry points, source modules, tests, docs, scripts, build artefacts).
3. Identify the primary language(s), package manager(s), runtime(s), and how to build / run / test the project. Use the existing manifests (package.json, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml, etc.) -- don't guess.
4. Identify the public surfaces: HTTP endpoints, CLIs, library exports, MCP tools, anything a consumer would touch.
5. Note anything fragile or interesting -- a custom build step, a database migration system, a code-generation step, a hot-reload watcher. Things a new contributor will trip on if they don't know.

Write the ABOUT.md to the current working directory. Keep it under 200 lines. Use H2 sections; lead each with the headline takeaway, not the explanation. Link to files with `file_path:line_number` syntax so a reader can click through.

When you're done, summarise the 3 most surprising things you learned -- things that would be useful to call out to me in chat regardless of whether they ended up in ABOUT.md.
